generated_comment: Arre, Naimat, tum bhi samjho. Yeh proposal kitna achha hai, har tarah se perfect hai. Ladka bhi achha hai, aur itne paise wale hain ki Sana ko kaam karne ki zaroorat hi nahi. Woh bas ghar pe rehne ki baat kar rahe hain. Kya nuksaan hai usme? Thode din ki naukri ke liye itna achha rishta chhodna sahi hai kya? Usse bolna padega, 'Soch, Sana, kya zyada important hai ek achhi shadi ya yeh job?'
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment frames a woman's career as secondary and dispensable when a 'good' marriage proposal (offering financial comfort and a settled partner) expects her to be a homemaker. It subtly pressures Sana to prioritize domestic roles over her professional aspirations by presenting it as a practical choice for her long-term benefit, devaluing her job as 'thode din ki naukri' (a job for a few days).
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 49. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your daughter Sana who is 25 has been working at a private company for two years and is doing well. A proposal has come for her from a good family. The boy is 28, settled, working in the Gulf. His family has said that they expect the wife to be at home after marriage Ñ they are comfortable financially and do not need her income. Sana has said she does not want to stop working. You are sitting with your wife discussing what to say to Sana and whether this proposal Ñ which ticks every other box Ñ should be declined because of this one issue or whether Sana needs to understand what she would be giving up by holding onto this one condition.
